2/12
INTRODUCTION

Introduction to Decision Trees

Decision trees are models for classification and regression tasks. They learn a hierarchy of if/else questions, leading to a decision.

1/10
SLIDE 04

Decision Trees > asking whether the animal has fins.

Decision trees are widely used models for classification and regression tasks. Essentially, they learn a hierarchy of if/else questions, leading to a decision. For continuous data, the questions are of the form 'Is feature i larger than value a?'

  • Learn a hierarchy of if/else questions📄 p.84
  • Classification and regression tasks📄 p.84
  • Questions for continuous data📄 p.85
  • Hierarchy of if/else questions📄 p.84
📄 Page 84-85
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-22. A decision tree to distinguish among several animals — Page 85
2/10
SLIDE 05

Decision Trees > ize the coefficients in the linear model ( Figure 2-28 ):

A decision tree to distinguish among several animals is illustrated in Figure 2-22. The visualization of the tree provides a great in-depth view of how the algorithm makes predictions.

  • Decision tree to distinguish among animals📄 p.85
  • Visualization of the tree📄 p.91
  • In-depth view of the algorithm📄 p.91
  • Decision tree of depth four📄 p.91
📄 Page 85-91
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-28. Feature importances computed from a decision tree learned on the Breast — Page 92
3/10
SLIDE 06

Ensembles of Decision Trees > ing the features in each split test. Let’s go into this process in more detail.

A main drawback of decision trees is that they tend to overfit the training data. Random forests are one way to address this issue.

  • Decision trees tend to overfit📄 p.97
  • Random forests address overfitting📄 p.97
  • Overfitting in decision trees📄 p.97
  • Random forests as a solution📄 p.97
📄 Page 97
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 3-2. Effect of scaling training and test data shown on the left together (center) and — Page 151
4/10
SLIDE 07

Ensembles of Decision Trees > might need to be very deep in order to fit the data well.

Ensembles of decision trees are models that combine the predictions of multiple decision trees. They can be used to improve the accuracy and robustness of decision trees.

  • Ensembles of decision trees📄 p.98
  • Combine predictions of multiple trees📄 p.98
  • Improve accuracy and robustness📄 p.98
  • Decision trees in ensembles📄 p.98
📄 Page 98
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-33. Decision boundaries found by five randomized decision trees and the deci‐ — Page 100
5/10
SLIDE 08

Ensembles of Decision Trees > ing the features in each split test. Let’s go into this process in more detail.

The algorithm for building an ensemble of decision trees is similar to the algorithm for building a single decision tree. However, the features used in each split test are selected randomly.

  • Algorithm for building an ensemble📄 p.97
  • Features selected randomly📄 p.97
  • Ensemble of decision trees📄 p.97
  • Random selection of features📄 p.97
📄 Page 97
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-2. Comparing linear regression and decision tree regression on binned features — Page 237
6/10
SLIDE 09

Ensembles of Decision Trees > is impractical to inspect them all, even if they are all of depth 1:

The number of trees in an ensemble can be adjusted to trade off between accuracy and computational cost. Increasing the number of trees can improve accuracy but also increases computation time.

  • Number of trees in an ensemble📄 p.102
  • Trade off between accuracy and cost📄 p.102
  • Increasing number of trees📄 p.102
  • Computation time increases📄 p.102
📄 Page 102
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-35. Feature importances computed from a gradient boosting classifier that was — Page 105
7/10
SLIDE 10

Model-Based Feature Selection > Decision trees and decision tree–based models provide a feature_importances_

Decision trees and decision tree-based models provide a feature_importances_ attribute, which can be used to select the most important features in a dataset.

  • Feature importance in decision trees📄 p.252
  • Feature_importances_ attribute📄 p.252
  • Selecting important features📄 p.252
  • Decision tree-based models📄 p.252
📄 Page 252
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-27. Visualization of the decision tree built on the Breast Cancer dataset — Page 90
8/10
SLIDE 11

Binning, Discretization, Linear Models, and Trees > described here.

Binning, discretization, linear models, and trees are different ways to represent data. The best way to represent data depends on the specific problem and dataset.

  • Ways to represent data📄 p.234
  • Binning, discretization, linear models, and trees📄 p.234
  • Best representation depends on the problem📄 p.234
  • Dataset-specific representation📄 p.234
📄 Page 234
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-1. Comparing linear regression and a decision tree on the wave dataset — Page 235
9/10
✦

Summary

Decision trees are models that learn a hierarchy of if/else questions to make predictions. They can be used for classification and regression tasks, and can be combined into ensembles to improve accuracy and robustness.

Decision trees
10/10
